items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Why are dried foods less prone to spoilage?
items.correct: Low water activity prevents microorganisms from growing.
items.misconception: Bacteria do not like dry textures.
items.why: Students think it's about texture preference. Microbes need available water. Drying reduces WATER ACTIVITY below the threshold microbes can use - no water = no metabolism = no spoilage.

items.q: Is organic food proven to be significantly more nutritious than non-organic food?
items.correct: No - organic refers to the farming method (no artificial pesticides/fertilisers), not guaranteed higher nutrient content.
items.misconception: Yes - organic food always contains far more vitamins and minerals.
items.random_wrong: Organic food contains no fat or sugar.
items.why: Students conflate 'organic' with 'healthier'. Organic certification controls how food is produced - restricting artificial pesticides, fertilisers and routine antibiotics. Reviews find little consistent difference in nutrient content.

items.q: Does locally produced food always have a lower carbon footprint than imported food?
items.correct: No - local food grown in heated greenhouses can emit more CO2 than imported field-grown food; transport is only part of the footprint.
items.misconception: Yes - fewer food miles always means a smaller carbon footprint.
items.random_wrong: Imported food has no carbon footprint because ships are free.
items.why: Students treat food miles as the whole story. Production method matters too: UK tomatoes grown in heated greenhouses in winter can have a bigger footprint than Spanish field tomatoes despite the lorry journey. Assess the whole life cycle.

items.q: How many portions of fruit and veg a day are recommended?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5. “10” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Eatwell Guide is from which country?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: UK. “USA” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Recommended portions of oily fish per week?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 portion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
